name: Brita Elite Replacement Filters
bestFor: Readers who already own a compatible Brita pitcher or dispenser and want the lowest-friction cartridge upgrade.
whyItFits: This is the cleanest interim move for homes that do not want a new installation. Brita positions Elite as its stronger lead-reduction cartridge and a longer-life upgrade over its standard filter.
watchout: This is a replacement filter, not a full setup. It only makes sense if the household already has a compatible Brita system and will keep cartridge changes on schedule.
evidenceNote: Brita says Elite fits all Brita pitchers and dispensers, removes 99% of lead, and is replaced about twice a year.

name: PUR PLUS Faucet Filtration System, Vertical
bestFor: Readers who want filtered drinking and cooking water on demand without waiting on a pitcher refill.
whyItFits: Faucet-mounted filtration is easy to explain on utility notice pages because it changes the daily habit less than a pitcher and gets filtered water right at the sink.
watchout: It still has faucet-fit constraints and a shorter cartridge window, so it is weaker for unusual fixtures or households that know they will miss regular filter swaps.
evidenceNote: PUR says this faucet system provides up to 100 gallons or about 3 months of typical use and is certified to reduce 70 contaminants, including lead.

name: ZeroWater 10 Cup Pitcher
bestFor: Readers who want a pitcher form factor but still want a stronger contaminant-reduction claim than a basic taste-and-odor filter.
whyItFits: This is a practical step up for households that prefer a portable pitcher and are willing to manage filter life more actively than they would with a faucet or under-sink system.
watchout: High-TDS tap water can shorten filter life, and pitcher flow can feel slower in bigger households, so this is not the best fit for heavy all-day kitchen demand.
evidenceNote: ZeroWater says its 5-stage filtration is IAPMO certified to reduce lead, PFOA/PFOS, chromium, and mercury.

name: Epic Smart Shield Under-Sink Water Filter System
bestFor: Homes that drink and cook from the same kitchen tap all day and want a longer-life setup with less cartridge swapping.
whyItFits: Under-sink filtration makes more sense once the household knows it needs a sturdier interim setup rather than the lightest-possible entry purchase. It is the strongest fit here for higher daily kitchen volume.
watchout: The upfront cost and install effort are higher than a pitcher or faucet mount, so it is a poor first move for short-term renters or readers who may move soon.
evidenceNote: Epic says Smart Shield is certified to NSF/ANSI 42, 53, and 401 and produces up to 651 gallons per filter.

name: Tap Score Lead and Copper Water Test
bestFor: Readers whose main question is whether lead or copper are showing up at the tap and who do not need a huge city-water panel yet.
whyItFits: This is the cleanest recommendation when the decision is narrow and the site is trying not to oversell a much broader test than the reader actually needs.
watchout: It will not answer the broader disinfection-byproduct or volatile-organic-compound questions that can matter after water-main work or a wider city-water concern.
evidenceNote: Tap Score says this kit is recommended for targeted lead and copper testing and includes certified laboratory analysis with quantified results.

name: Tap Score Essential City Water Test
bestFor: Readers on utility water who want a broader baseline than a lead-only kit without immediately paying for the largest panel.
whyItFits: This sits in the middle: broad enough for a real baseline, still narrower than the advanced package, and easier to justify when the reader is checking a known utility-water concern.
watchout: It is still not the best pick when the concern includes a bigger range of byproducts, VOCs, or a more exhaustive city-water read after repeated problems.
evidenceNote: Tap Score says the Essential City Water Test includes 49 analytes with free shipping both ways and a 5-business-day turnaround.

name: Tap Score Advanced City Water Test
bestFor: Readers who want one thorough lab-backed answer after a notice, repeated water-quality concern, or post-replacement comparison.
whyItFits: This is the stronger recommendation when the reader clearly wants a full baseline instead of piecemeal testing and would act differently if a wider set of contaminants appears.
watchout: It costs more than the narrow kits, so it is overkill unless the reader will actually use the broader result set to decide on treatment, follow-up testing, or escalation.
evidenceNote: Tap Score says the Advanced City Water Test covers 111 analytes, including metals, VOCs, and disinfection byproducts, with step-by-step sampling instructions.
